title: Workbook — The Dignity of the Human Person
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Section 1: Remembering the Essentials

Answer from your memory of the lesson, then check your answers against the Catechism.

  1. What does the book of Genesis say about the creation of the human being? Quote the verse.
  2. What three faculties make us «in the image of God,» according to the Catechism?
  3. What consequences did original sin have on human dignity — and what did it NOT do?
  4. What does it mean that the human being is a unity of body and soul?
  5. What did St. Leo the Great tell newly baptized Christians about their dignity?
  6. According to Gaudium et Spes 22, what does Christ do for man?
  7. Name the four pillars of Catholic social teaching that flow from human dignity.
  8. What four modern errors attack human dignity, according to the lesson?

Section 2: For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions — only honest ones.

1. What really makes you feel valuable? Where does your sense of self-worth come from?

Think about the voices you hear when you evaluate yourself. Are they the world's voices or God's voice? What would change if you grounded your self-worth in being created in the image of God?

 

 

 

2. Is there someone you find it difficult to treat with dignity? Why?

It could be someone who has hurt you, someone whose views you despise, or someone you simply ignore because they seem unimportant. What would it mean to see the image of God in them?

 

 

 

3. The wound of original sin. In what area of your life do you feel an «interior division» most strongly?

St. Paul described it: «I do not do the good I want, but the evil I do not want is what I do» (Rom 7:19). Where do you experience that tension between who you want to be and what you actually do?

 

 

 

4. What would change in your life if you truly believed you were a child of God with infinite dignity?

Be concrete: a decision you would make, a conversation you would have, a habit you would change. Name at least two things.

 

 

 

5. «Christ reveals man to man himself.» What do you learn about yourself when you look at Jesus?

Jesus is perfectly free, perfectly self-giving, perfectly merciful. What does his way of being human teach you about your own humanity and vocation?

 

 

 

6. How can you recognize and defend the dignity of the people around you this week?

In your family, your workplace, your school, your community. Name three concrete actions.

 

 

 

Section 3: Practical Activities

Activity A. The Mirror of Dignity

Stand in front of a mirror and look into your own eyes. Say aloud: «You are the image of God. God loves you. You have infinite dignity.» Do this every day this week (even if it feels awkward at first). After seven days, write here how it has affected you:

 

 

 

 

Activity B. The Portrait of Dignity

Choose a person you find difficult to treat with respect (a difficult coworker, a conflicting family member, someone with different opinions). Write three things that God — who sees beyond appearances — would see in them that you do not see:

1.  

2.  

3.  

Activity C. The Dignity Detector

This week, every time you encounter another person — especially someone you normally ignore (the bus driver, the cleaning staff, the person begging on the street) — say silently to yourself: «Image of God.» At the end of the week, write what changed in you:

 

 

 

Activity D. A Concrete Action

Do one concrete thing this week that honors your dignity as a child of God. Choose one:

  • Take good care of your body (exercise, rest, nourishing food)
  • Dedicate real time to silent prayer
  • Say no to something that degrades you (a habit, a website, a conversation)
  • Help someone in need without wanting anything in return
  • Write a letter to one person thanking them for something

Which one did you choose? What happened when you did it?

 

 

 

Activity E. Saint Connection: St. Josephine Bakhita

Read about St. Josephine Bakhita (search online or read her brief biography). She was kidnapped, enslaved, tortured, branded — and yet said: «I am definitively loved.»

Answer: What does her story teach you about where dignity really comes from? How does it help you understand that dignity is indestructible?

 

 

 

Activity F. «Wonder» Connection

If possible, watch the film Wonder (2017) this week — or at minimum watch the trailer. Auggie Pullman has a severe facial deformity. In what specific moments of the film (or from what you imagine) does his dignity shine through despite how others treat him? What does «choose kind» have to do with human dignity?

 

 

 

Section 4: Memory Verse

«God created man in his own image; in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them.»

— Genesis 1:27

Write it here from memory (cover the text above and try first):

 

 

 

Section 5: Prayer for This Week

Lord, you who created me in your image,
help me to know myself as you see me.
Heal the wounds that darken your image in me,
and teach me to see your face in every person I meet.
Through Christ, who reveals me to myself,
may I live always according to my dignity as your child,
and never forget that I was created by love, for love,
destined for eternal beatitude with you.
Amen.

Your own prayer: Write a short prayer asking for the grace to live according to your dignity this week.

 

 

 

Section 6: One Thing I'll Remember

You studied a lot today — imago Dei, body and soul, original sin, Christ's redemption, Catholic social teaching, modern errors, St. Josephine Bakhita, Mother Teresa, the Theology of the Body. If someone asked you tomorrow: «What did you learn about human dignity?» — what would you say in one sentence?

 

 

 

Print Workbook
